State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662       322-5660       322-5901       322-6441       322-6444


November 24, 1982


Allen Grimes
City Attorney
City of Atascadero
P.O. Box 749
Atascadero, CA  93423

                                        Re:  Your Advice Request of
                                             11/10/82
                                             Our No. A-82-188

Dear Mr. Grimes:

     Thank you for your recent letter requesting our assistance.
Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we
cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18329(b) (copy enclosed).  However, we will
endeavor to respond generally to your questions.

     The possibility of financial benefit to either councilmember
must be measured against the standards established in the
Commission's regulation defining "material financial effect" as
used in Government Code Section 87103.  Regulation 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18702 provides that guidance and a copy is
enclosed.  As to the park tenant, subdivision (b)(3)(A) would be
applicable.  As to the park owner, subdivision (b)(1) or (b)(2)
would apply.

     Assuming that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will
be a material financial effect on the councilmember(s) in
question, then the issue will turn on whether the effect on the
councilmember's financial interest is distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.  (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703, copy enclosed.)  The Ferraro and Overstreet Opinions
issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in
this regard.

Allen Grimes
November 24, 1982
Page 2


     While we have been provided with no specific facts on the
"public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home
park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as
a significant segment of the public generally.  The numerosity
and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear
to be present.

     With respect to the park tenant, our advice letter to
Paul H. Morgan, A-81-507, 12/2/81, which you have not mentioned
in your request, may provide you with some additional guidance,
a copy is enclosed for your review.  However, it should be
remembered that there is no fixed percentage cut-off for
determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public
generally as other factors could influence the cut-off level.

     Lastly, without some specific facts we are unable to assist
you further in your endeavor to distinguish among our advice
letters, A-81-503, A-82-036 and A-82-063.

     If you would like further assistance in this regard, I may
be reached at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Robert B. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

Re: A-82-188

Dear Mr. Grimes:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert Leidigh, an attorney
in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission.  If you have any questions about your advice
request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

Atascadero

CITY ATTORNEY

FIRE DEPARTMENT
6005 LEWIS AVENUE
ATASCADERO, CALIFORNIA 93422
PHONE: (805) 466-2141

November 10, 1982


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95814

Subject:  Disqualification of a Council Member
          Who is a Mobile Home Park Resident

Dear Sirs:

We have difficulty distinguishing between the opinions rendered in your Files
No. A-81-503, A-82-036, and A-82-063. We respectfully request an opinion of
the Commission on the following question:

A member of the Atascadero City Council resides in a mobile home park located
on private land within the City. There has been presented to the Council by
a mobile home owners group within the City a proposal and recommendation to
the Council to enact a mobile home park rent stabilization ordinance. While
the form of the ordinance may vary in its consideration, it is conceivable
that it could impose a limitation on the increase of mobile home park rents,
which would appear to be of potential financial benefit to the Council member.
The question then arises as to whether the Council member may participate in
discussions on this matter before the Council and vote on any ordinance pro-
posed for action. It is conceivable, of course, that any such ordinance would
not be sustained if challenged in court on the ground that it is unconstitu-
tional because it does not guarantee a fair return to the owner's investment.

On the other hand, it is conceivable that other members of the Council have
an ownership interest in a mobile home park located within the City. It is
conceivable that if any rent stabilization ordinance is adopted, those finan-
cial interests would be substantially affected by the adoption of the ordinance
through reduction in rents. Thus, the same questions exists, may such a Council
member participate and/or vote in an action relating to mobile home park rent
stabilization.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

ALLEN GRIMES
City Attorney

August 17, 1982

Janice McLean, Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Re:  City Treasurer of Atascadero

Dear Janice:

In connection with your study of the matter, I am enclosing for your edification a copy of City of Atascadero Ordinance No. 49 and Government Code §§ 41001, 41002, 41006, 41007, 53630, 53631, 53635, 53637, 53643, 53649, and 53653, and make reference to the citations to the Public Deposit Act, which is found in Government Code § 53600, et seq.

Our City Finance Director, Ralph Dowell, advised me by telephone yesterday that he knows of no existence of a bidding system other than what might be contained in the provisions of the Public Deposit Act. Mr. Dowell has served as the appointed City Treasurer of Atascadero from its incorporation in 1979 until June of 1982, when Steve Rizzuto was elected City Treasurer.

Moreover, I do not know of any bidding procedure or guidelines for selecting depositories for surplus city funds that have been adopted by the City Council since the incorporation of the City. Any such guidelines adopted by the City Treasurer, of course, would simply be self-serving and subject to change.

Even if the Council had adopted such guidelines, it has been held in the contract field that the fact that a contract is fair, or even advantageous to the city, or has been let to the lowest bidder, is immaterial. (Stockton Plumbing & Supply Co. v. Wheeler (1924) 68 C.A. 952.)

Your examination of the provisions of the Public Deposit Act will reveal the extent to which discretion has been placed in the City Treasurer with regard to the deposit of public funds. I call your attention to the annotations of decisions and opinions of the Attorney General relating to the Public Deposit Act.

Very truly yours,

ALLEN GRIMES
City Attorney

ADMINISTRATION BUILDING
POST OFFICE BOX 747
ATASCADERO, CALIFORNIA 93422

PHONE (805) 466-8000

FIRE DEPARTMENT
6005 LEWIS AVENUE
ATASCADERO, CA 93422
(805) 466-2141


August 20, 1982


Janis Shank McLean
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Your Letter of Advice, No. A-82-124

Dear Ms. McLean:

I have just read a copy of your response to the request for advice by Steven
M. Rizzuto, City Treasurer of Atascadero, dated August 13, 1982, together with
a copy of his letter to you dated August 16, 1982.

I want to say that I heartily agree with your conclusion that the Political
Reform Act does not prevent Mr. Rizzuto from acting as treasurer while he is
employed by Fidelity Savings. However, I am strongly of the opinion that the
Act requires Mr. Rizzuto to disqualify himself from making or participating
in decisions which select Fidelity Savings as a depository of surplus city
funds.

I do not believe there is any sound basis upon which it can be contended that
the treasurer is legally required to select his own depository as a place for
the deposit of surplus city funds.

I think it is only honest to advise you that, irrespective of any opinions
that otherwise might be furnished to Mr. Rizzuto, the above conclusion will
be the opinion of this office. More than anything else, I make this conclusion on the basis of Government Code § 1090 and the common law against unlawful conflicts of interest, and all of those cases which historically have
pointed out that a person may not lawfully serve two masters. No amount of
intellectual jargon can avoid the conclusion that a person should not lawfully
be allowed to be in the position of selecting his own company as a recipient
of a benefit from the city when he is treasurer.

Very truly yours,

ALLEN GRIMES
City Attorney

ORDINANCE NO. 49

AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF ATASCADERO ADDING
CHAPTER 5 TO TITLE 2 OF THE ATASCADERO
MUNICIPAL CODE ESTABLISHING THE
OFFICE OF CITY TREASURER


The Atascadero City Council does ordain as follows:

Section 1. Chapter 5 is added to Title 2 of the Atascadero Municipal Code to read as follows:

TITLE 2. ADMINISTRATION

Chapter 5. City Treasurer

2-5.201. Creation and functions. The office of the City Treasurer is hereby confirmed as provided in Section 36501 and 36502 of the Government Code of the State. The City Treasurer shall perform such duties as are prescribed by Section 41001 through 41007 of the Government Code of the State. The City Treasurer shall perform such other duties consistent with this Code as may be required of him by the Council.

The principal functions of the City Treasurer shall be to receive and safely keep all public funds coming into his hands as treasurer, and to comply with all laws governing the deposit and securing of public funds and the handling of trust funds in his possession.

2-5.202. Deputies. The City Treasurer may appoint deputies for whose acts he and his bondsmen are responsible, but in doing so, shall not incur any indebtedness or liability on behalf of the City except as authorized by the City Council.

2-5.203. Compensation. The City Treasurer shall receive such compensation and expense allowance as the Council shall from time to time determine by resolution or ordinance. Such compensation shall be a proper charge against such funds of the City as the Council shall designate.

2-5.204. Coordination with Director of Finance. The City Treasurer shall coordinate his activities with the Director of Finance to assure availability of funds in response to demands for payment against City financial resources. The City Treasurer shall not withhold making available funds to the Finance Director for payment of materials, equipment, goods or services duly authorized by Council through the adopted budget or by Council approval by ordinance, resolution or motion when requests for such funds are duly authorized and presented by the Director of Finance.

Ordinance No. - City Treasurer

2-5.205. Investment Policy. In addition to the requirement, duties and obligations imposed upon the City Treasurer by the State of California Government Code concerning the investment of City monies, the City Treasurer shall attempt to secure the maximum the rise of return on invested uncommitted City funds consistent with assuring availability of funds to meet the City's financial obligations and consistent with statutory investment limitations. In order to assure compliance with this requirement, the City Treasurer shall closely coordinate the investment of funds with the Director of Finance to assure the ability of the City to meet its obligations.

Section 2. Ordinance No. 6, adopted on July 9, 1979, shall be repealed effective with assumption of office by a duly elected City Treasurer, but shall constitute transition provisions pending the election of such a Treasurer.

Section 3. The City Clerk shall cause this ordinance to be published once within fifteen (15) days after its passage in the Atascadero News, a newspaper of general circulation, printed and circulated in this City in accordance with Section 36933 of the Government Code; shall certify the adoption of this ordinance; and shall cause this ordinance and its certification, to be entered in the Book of Ordinances of this City.

Section 4. This ordinance shall go into effect and be in full force and effect at 12:01 a.m. on the thirty-first (31st) day after passage, but shall not repeal the transition provisions of Ordinance No. 6.

The foregoing ordinance was adopted and ordered published at a meeting of the Council held on February 9, 1982, by the following vote:

AYES: Councilmen Highland, Mackey, Nelson, and Mayor Wilkins

NOES: None

ABSENT: Councilman Stover

ROBERT J.WILKINS, JR., Mayor

ATTEST:

MURRAY L. WARDEN, City Clerk

APPROVED AS TO FORM:

ALLEN GRIMES, City Attorney

§ 41000          GOVERNMENT OF CITIES

§ 41001.   Money; keeping. The city treasurer shall receive and
safely keep all money coming into his hands as treasurer. (Added
Stats.1949, c. 79, p. 215, § 1.)


§ 41002.   Deposit and security of funds; compliance with laws.
He shall comply with all laws governing the deposit and securing of
public funds and the handling of trust funds in his possession. (Added
Stats.1949, c. 79, p. 215, § 1.)


§ 41006.   Deputies; appointment. The city treasurer may appoint deputies for whose acts he and his bondsmen are responsible.
(Added Stats.1949, c. 79, p. 215, § 1.)


§ 41007.   Deputies; term; compensation. The deputies shall
hold office at the pleasure of the city treasurer and receive such compensation as is provided by the legislative body. (Added Stats.1949,
c. 79, p. 216, § 1.)




FR:8/17/82

§ 53630                    GOVERNMENT CODE

§ 53630.   Definitions

As used in this article:

(a) "Local agency" means county, city, city and county, or other public agency
or corporation.

(b) "Treasurer" means treasurer of the local agency.

(c) "Depository" means a state or national bank or state or federal savings and
loan association in which the moneys of a local agency are deposited.

(d) "Agent of depository" means a trust company or trust department of a state
or national bank located in this state, including the trust department of a depository where authorized, and the Federal Home Loan Bank of San Francisco.

(e) "Security" means any of the eligible securities or obligations listed in Section 53651.

(f) "Pooled securities" means eligible securities held by an agent of depository
for a depository and securing deposits of one or more local agencies.

(g) "Administrator" means the Administrator of Local Agency Security of the
State of California.


§ 53631.   Deposits to pay principal and interest of bonds. Under such conditions as the treasurer of a local agency fixes with the approval of the legislative body, he may deposit money necessary to pay the principal and interest of bonds in a bank within or without the State at the place where they are payable. This article does not apply
to deposits for such purposes. (Added Stats.1949, c. 81, p. 290, § 1.)

§ 53635                    GOVERNMENT CODE

§ 53635. Funds of local agency; deposit or investment
As far as possible, all money belonging to, or in the custody of, a local agency,
including money paid to the treasurer or other official to pay the principal, inter-
est, or maturities of bonds, shall be deposited for safekeeping in state or national
banks or state or federal savings and loan associations in the state selected by the
treasurer or other official having the legal custody of the money, or may be invest-
ed in the following unless otherwise directed by the legislative body pursuant to
Section 53601:

(a) Bonds issued by it, including bonds payable solely out of the revenues from a
revenue-producing property owned, controlled, or operated by it or by a department,
board, agency, or authority thereof.

(b) United States Treasury notes, bonds, bills, or certificates of indebtedness, or
those for which the faith and credit of the United States are pledged for the pay-
ment of principal and interest.

(c) Registered state warrants or treasury notes or bonds of this state, including
bonds payable solely out of the revenues from a revenue-producing property owned,
controlled, or operated by the state or by a department, board, agency, or authority
thereof.

(d) Bonds, notes, warrants, or other evidences of indebtedness of any local agency
within this state, including bonds payable solely out of the revenues from a reve-
nue-producing property owned, controlled, or operated by the local agency, or by a
department, board, agency, or authority thereof.

(e) Obligations issued by banks for cooperatives, federal land banks, federal Inter-
mediate credit banks, federal home loan banks, the Federal Home Loan Bank, the
Tennessee Valley Authority, or in obligations, participations, or other instruments
of or issued by, or fully guaranteed as to principal and interest by, the Federal Na-
tional Mortgage Association; or in guaranteed portions of Small Business Administra-
tion notes; or in obligations, participations, or other instruments of or issued by
a Federal agency or a United States government-sponsored enterprise.

(f) Bills of exchange or time drafts drawn on and accepted by a commercial
bank, otherwise known as bankers acceptances, which are eligible for purchase by
the Federal Reserve System. Purchases of bankers acceptances may not exceed 270
days maturity or 30 percent of the agency's surplus funds which may be invested
pursuant to this section.

The provisions of this subdivision shall not preclude a municipal utility district
from investing any surplus money in its treasury in any manner authorized by the
Municipal Utility District Act.

(g) Commercial paper of "prime" quality of the highest ranking or of the highest
letter and numerical rating as provided for by Moody's Investors Service, Inc., or
Standard and Poor's Corporation. Eligible paper is further limited to issuing corpo-
rations that are organized and operating within the United States and having to-
tal assets in excess of five hundred million dollars ($500,000,000) and having an "A"
or higher rating for the issuer's debentures, other than commercial paper, as pro-
vided for by Moody's Investors Service, Inc., or Standard and Poor's Corporation.
Purchases of eligible commercial paper may not exceed 180 days maturity nor rep-
resent more than 10 percent of the outstanding paper of an issuing corporation.
Purchases of commercial paper may not exceed 15 percent of the agency's surplus
money which may be invested pursuant to this section.

No investment in securities described in this subdivision shall be made by a local
agency having a population of less than 100,000.

(h) Negotiable certificates of deposits issued by a nationally or state-chartered
bank or a state or federal savings and loan association. Purchases of negotiable
certificates of deposits may not exceed 15 percent of the agency's surplus money
which may be invested pursuant to this section. For purposes of this section, nego-
tiable certificates of deposits do not come within the provisions of Article 2 (com-
mencing with Section 53630) of Chapter 4 of Part 1 of Division 2 of Title 5.

No investment in securities described in this subdivision shall be made by a local
agency having a population of less than * * * 50,000.

(i) Investments in repurchase agreements or reverse repurchase agreements of
any securities authorized by this section. For purposes of this section, the term
"repurchase agreement" means a purchase of securities by the local agency pursu-
ant to an agreement by which the seller will repurchase such securities on or be-
fore a specified date and for a specified amount.

For purposes of this section, the term "reverse repurchase agreement" means a
sale of securities by the local agency pursuant to an agreement by which the local
agency will repurchase such securities on or before a specified date and for a speci-
fied amount.

Investment in a reverse repurchase agreement shall be made only upon prior ap-
proval of the legislative body of the local agency.

GOVERNMENT CODE

§ 53637. Selection of depositary; interest

The money shall be deposited in any depositary selected from those banks and
those savings and loan associations agreeing to pay the highest rate of interest
* * *.

§ 53643. Deposit for definite term

The treasurer may deposit any part of the money for a definite term agreed
upon between the treasurer and the depositary. * * *

§ 53649. Contracts with depositories; contents; filing

The treasurer is responsible for the safekeeping of * * * money in his custody and shall enter into any contract with a depositary which in his judgment
is to the public advantage. The depositary, and the agent of depositary to the extent the agent of depositary has been notified of deposits and the amount thereof,
are responsible for securing moneys deposited pursuant to such a contract in accordance with Section 53652. One copy of such such contract shall be filed with
the auditor, controller, secretary or corresponding officer of the local agency. The
contract shall:
(a) Fix the duration of deposits.
(b) Fix the interest rate, if any.
(c) Provide conditions for withdrawal and repayment.
(d) Provide for placement of pooled securities in a named agent of depositary
in accordance with Section 53656.
(e) Grant authority for agent of depositary to place securities for safekeeping
in accordance with Section 53659.
(f) Set forth in accordance with Section 53665 the conditions upon which the
administrator shall order pooled securities converted into money for the benefit
of the local agency, and the procedure therefor.
(g) Provide for compliance in all respects with the provisions of this article and
other applicable provisions of law.

§ 53653. Waiver of security

When in his discretion local conditions so warrant, the treasurer may waive security for such portion of any deposits as is insured pursuant to federal law, notwithstanding this article.

90                    ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS                    [VOL. 21

since the adopted regulation, both are in conflict with the express terms of
section 60 of the Act. Section 134 is a regulation in excess of the power of the
Board and is, therefore, void as to that excess. The rule-making power of the
Board, being derived from section 38 of the Act, is only a power to make rules
not inconsistent with any of the provisions of the Act itself. Government Code
section 11374, which is a codification of the common law rule, states:

    " . . . no regulation adopted is valid or effective unless consistent
and not in conflict with the statute and reasonably necessary to, effectuate
the purpose of the statute." (Whitcomb Hotel, Inc. v. California Employ-
ment Commission, 24 Cal. 2d 753; Blatz Brewing Co. v. Collins, 69 Cal.
App. 2d 659; Tux Ginger Ale Co., Ltd. v. Davii, 12 Cal. App. 2d 73.)

As stated by the Supreme Court, at page 757, in the Whitcomb Hotel case,
supra: " . . . An administrative officer may not make a rule or regulation that
alters or enlarges the terms of a legislative enactment. . . . "

    For the foregoing reasons, it is our opinion that section 134 of Title 4 of
the California Administrative Code, to the extent that it purports to authorize the
delivery to an on-sale beer licensee upon a Sunday, is ineffective and invalid.

                                    ————◆————

                    Opinion No. 53-24—March 4, 1953

    SUBJECT: CITY OF MODESTO may lawfully, under its charter, deposit funds
in a bank of which one of the city Councilmen is a member of the Board of
Directors and an owner of less than five per cent of the oustanding capital
stock.

    Requested by: ASSEMBLYMAN, 30th DISTRICT.

    Opinion by: EDMUND G. BROWN, Attorney General.
                Willard A. Shank, Deputy.

    The Honorable Ralph M. Brown, Member of the California Assembly, has
asked the following question:

    "May the City of Modesto lawfully deposit public funds in a bank
of which a Modesto City Councilman is a member of the Board of
Directors and an owner of less than 5% of the outstanding capital stock
of the bank?"

    Our conclusions may be summarized as follows:

    The City Treasurer of the City of Modesto may deposit public funds in a
bank in which a Modesto City Councilman is a director and stockholder owning
less than 5% of the outstanding capital stock.

                            ANALYSIS

    Government Code section 1090 provides that state, county, and city officers
shall not be interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or
any body or board of which they are members. Violations are punishable as crimes

MARCH 1953]

ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS

91

and subject the offender to removal from office (Gov. Code, secs. 1097, 36528).
These provisions are declaratory of the well established common law principle of
public policy which demands that public officers shall be free of personal temptations in the performance of their official duties (Stockton Plumbing and Supply
Co. v. Wheeler (1924), 68 Cal. App. 592, 229 Pac. 1020). As a general rule, any
direct or indirect interest of an officer in the subject of a contract with a public
body is sufficient to taint the contract with illegality, if the interest is such as to
affect the judgment and conduct of the officer either in the making of the contract
or its performance (County of Marin v. Messner (1941), 44 Cal. App. 2d 577,
112 Pac.2d 731).

In addition to these provisions, the Charter of the City of Modesto (section
905, Assembly Concurrent Resolution 31, Ch. 46, Stats. 1951) provides that no
employee of the city shall be financially interested in a contract, sale, purchase or
lease or transfer of real property to which the city is a party or be employed by or
have an interest in any organization or firm or corporation having a contract with
the city. The Charter provision further provides that an interest of less than 5%
of the stock of a corporation shall not be deemed a "financial interest" under the
section.

There may be some question as to which of the above provisions of law is
applicable in the present case, though the provisions are essentially the same,
with the exception that a stock interest of less than 5% is not considered culpable
under the charter section. Since our conclusion would be the same under either
of the provisions, it is unnecessary to discuss a possible conflict between the
general law and the charter provision at this time.

No mention is made in the Charter for the deposit of public funds of the
city and the provisions of the Government Code relating to deposit of funds by
local agencies (sections 53630-53681) would apply (Clouse v. City of San Diego,
159 Cal. 434, 115 Pac. 573).

The City Treasurer is authorized by section 53635 of the Government Code to
deposit public funds in national or state banks and "money so deposited is deemed
to be in the treasury of the local agency" (section 53636).

The precise question presented, therefore, is whether the fact that a councilman
is a director and holder of less than a 5% interest in a bank prohibits the selection
of that bank as a depository of city funds by the City Treasurer.

The Attorney General has held that the fact that a person is a legislator does
not prevent his entry into contracts of sale with the State of California or its
agencies, except that he may not enter into a contract with the legislature itself
(14 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 78); that a head court house gardener who owned a
private nursery was not disqualified from selling nursery supplies to the county
of which he was an employee because of the discretion vested in the county purchasing agent to conclude contracts for the county (3 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 188);
that a County Supervisor is not precluded from contracting for construction work
with a school district since the contracts for school buildings or school construction

92                    ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS                [VOL. 21

are let by Boards of School Trustees without control or supervision of the County
Board of Supervisors (17 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 44). The significant fact in each
of these opinions is the independent status of the party contracting on behalf of
the governmental agency.

The selection of the depository in the instant case is in the discretion of the
City Treasurer and not subject to the supervision or control of the City Council.
It is true that the council is responsible for the appointment and removal of the
Treasurer, but the relationship between one member of the council and the
Treasurer is no less remote than that of a legislator and an agency of the State
Government.

We are of the opinion that the public funds of the City of Modesto may be
deposited by the City Treasurer in a bank in which a councilman of the city is a
director and a stockholder with less than 5% of the outstanding capital stock.

——————◆◆——————

Opinion No. 52-175—March 5, 1953

SUBJECT: STATE EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM—Members of, already
retired under, are not barred from service of the State or of a contracting
agency, provided that they meet the requirements of reinstatement from
retirement; if such reinstatement is effected, such employee may be paid and
accept salary or emoluments of office with the state or the contracting agency.

Requested by: BOARD OF ADMINISTRATION, STATE EMPLOYEES'
RETIREMENT SYSTEM.

Opinion by: EDMUND G. BROWN, Attorney General.
John Morris, Deputy.

The Board of Administration of the State Employees' Retirement System has
asked whether one retired under the State Employees' Retirement System may be
elected to office in the service of the State or of a contracting agency, and if elected,
whether he may be paid the salary of such office.

The conclusions reached may be summarized as follows:

If otherwise qualified, a person retired under the State Employees' Retirement
System is not barred from office by retirement alone, provided that he meet the
requirements of reinstatement from retirement; and, reinstatement effected and
retirement allowance terminated, he may be paid and accept the salary or emolu-
ments of office.

ANALYSIS

Section 20060 of the Government Code was amended in 1949 to read as
follows: "Except as provided in section 21300 a person shall not be paid for any
service rendered by him to the State or contracting agency after his retirement is\*
effective and while he is entitled to receive a retirement allowance under this
system."

Fidelity Savings
and Loan Association
of San Francisco
A FEDERAL SAVINGS AND LOAN ASSOCIATION


August 16, 1982

Janis Shank McLean
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

re: Your Letter of Advice, No. A-82-124

Dear Ms. McLean:

I appreciate the manner and clarity in which you responded to my
previous letter requesting advice. After discussing your letter
with you today, I believe that we should look further into the
application of Government Code Section 18701, as I believe I am
legally required to participate in the decision process which is
subject to our discussion of "conflict of interest".

I am enclosing for your review Ordinance No. 49 of the City of
Atascadero, adding Chapter 5 to title 2 of the Atascadero Municipal
Code which established the office of City Treasurer. Further,
I believe there exists no means at my disposal to secure an
independent alternative source of decision consistent with the
purposes and terms of the statute and ordinance authorizing the
decision.

As you suggested in your response letter, paragraph 9, the bidding
system removes most of my involvement in determining whether Fidelity
Savings, or any other association, is chosen as one of the institutions
to be used for our investment of reserve public funds, and my contractual
arrangements with all depositories are principally ministerial. However,
the investment policy as established in Title 2 - 5.205 of the
Atascadero Municipal Code does require the Treasurer's attempt to secure
the maximum rate of return on invested uncommitted City funds and
more broadly Government Code 53637, and 53682. Subjective evaluation
on the part of the Treasurer will enter into the decision process if,
in the opinion of the Treasurer, the deposit of funds will not be to
the public advantage. In this situation, security of deposited funds
may be taken into consideration and 'rate v. risk' judgement may be
involved. Under Atas. 2-5.202, and GC 41007, any deputy shall hold
office at the pleasure of the city treasurer,...which I contrue to mean
to follow the policy established by code, ordinance, and policy then
established by the Treasurer.

7109 EL CAMINO REAL • ATASCADERO, CALIFORNIA 93422-9990 • 805-466-6550
Board of Directors: Harold R. Walt, Father Timothy L. McDonnell, S.J., J. Ralph Stone, Edward H. Barker, Sherman Miller

Janis McLean
August 16, 1982
page 2.

I hope that this information will be of further use to you in
helping determine the ultimate question as to whether I can
effectively participate in the decision making process in
the investing inactive public reserves.

Should you have need for further information, please feel free
to contact me. I have supplied our city attorney, Allen Grimes,
with copies of your letter dated August 13, 1982, and this
additional request.

Very cordially yours,

[signature]

Steven M. Rizzuto
Atascadero City Treasurer


cc Allen Grimes, City Attorney

ORDINANCE NO. 49

AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF ATASCADERO ADDING
CHAPTER 5 TO TITLE 2 OF THE ATASCADERO
MUNICIPAL CODE ESTABLISHING THE
OFFICE OF CITY TREASURER

The Atascadero City Council does ordain as follows:

Section 1. Chapter 5 is added to Title 2 of the Atascadero Municipal Code to read as follows:

TITLE 2. ADMINISTRATION ·

Chapter 5. City Treasurer ·

2-5.201. Creation and functions. The office of the City Treasurer is hereby confirmed as provided in Section 36501 and 36502 of the Government Code of the State. The City Treasurer shall perform such duties as are prescribed by section 41001 through 41007 of the Government Code of the State. The City Treasurer shall perform such other duties consistent with this Code as may be required of him by the Council.

The principal functions of the City Treasurer shall be to receive and safely keep all public funds coming into his hands as treasurer, and to comply with all laws governing the deposit and securing of public funds and the handling of trust funds in his possession.

2-5.202. Deputies. The City Treasurer may appoint deputies for whose acts he and his bondsmen are responsible; but in doing so, shall not incur any indebtedness or liability on behalf of the City except as authorized by the City Council.

2-5.203. Compensation. The City Treasurer shall receive such compensation and expense allowance as the Council shall from time to time determine by resolution or ordinance. Such compensation shall be a proper charge against such funds of the City as the Council shall designate.

2-5.204. Coordination with Director of Finance. The City Treasurer shall coordinate his activities with the Director of Finance to assure availability of funds in response to demands for payment against City financial resources. The City Treasurer shall not withhold making available funds to the Finance Director for payment of materials, equipment, goods or services duly authorized by Council through the adopted budget or by Council approval by ordinance, resolution or motion when requests for such funds are duly authorized and presented by the Director of Finance.

Ordinance No. - City Treasurer

2-5.205. Investment Policy. In addition to the requirement, duties and obligations imposed upon the City Treasurer by the State of California Government Code concerning the investment of City monies, the City Treasurer shall attempt to secure the maximum the rate of return on invested uncommitted City funds consistent with assuring availability of funds to meet the City's financial obligations and consistent with statutory investment limitations. In order to assure compliance with this requirement, the City Treasurer shall closely coordinate the investment of funds with the Director of Finance to assure the ability of the City to meet its obligations.

Section 2. Ordinance No. 6, adopted on July 9, 1979, shall be repealed effective with assumption of office by a duly elected City Treasurer, but shall constitute transition provisions pending the election of such a Treasurer.

Section 3. The City Clerk shall cause this ordinance to be published once within fifteen (15) days after its passage in the Atascadero News, a newspaper of general circulation, printed, published and circulated in this City in accordance with Section 36933 of the Government Code; shall certify the adoption of this ordinance; and shall cause this ordinance and its certification, to be entered in the Book of Ordinances of this City.

Section 4. This ordinance shall go into effect and be in full force and effect at 12:01 a.m. on the thirty-first (31st) day after passage, but shall not repeal the transition provisions of Ordinance No. 6.

The foregoing ordinance was adopted and ordered published at a meeting of the Council held on February 9_______, 1982, by the following vote:

AYES: Councilmen Highland, Mackey, Nelson, and Mayor Wilkins

NOES: None

ABSENT: Councilman Stover

ROBERT J. WILKINS, JR., Mayor

ATTEST:

MURRAY L. WARDEN, City Clerk

APPROVED AS TO FORM:

ALLEN GRIMES, City Attorney

The numerousity 
and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear

sections.conclusion: Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, the possibility of financial benefit must be measured against the standards established in the Commission's regulation defining 'material financial effect' as used in Government Code Section 87103 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702). For a park tenant, subdivision (b)(3)(A) would be applicable; for a park owner, subdivision (b)(1) or (b)(2) would apply. Assuming a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, the issue turns on whether the effect on the councilmember's financial interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703). It is unlikely that a mobile home park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public generally, as the numerosity and diversity requirements do not appear to be present.
sections.analysis:
Government Code section 1090 provides that state, county, and city officers " 
shall not be interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or 
any body or board of which they are members. Violations are punishable as crimes 


MARCH 1953] 
ATfORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
91 
and subject the offender to removal from office (Gov. Code, secs. 1097, 36528). 
These provisions are declaratory of rhe well established common law principle of 
public policy which demands that public officers shall be free of personal tempta-
tions in rhe performance of their official duties (Stockton Plumbing and Supply 
Co. v. Wheeler (1924), 68 Cal. App. 592, 229 Pac. 1020). As a general rule, any 
direer or indirect interest of an officer in the subject of a contract with a public 
- -
body is sufficient to taint the contract with illegality, if the interest is such as to 
weer rhe judgment and conduct of the officer either in the making of the contmct 
or its performance (County of Marin v. Messner (1941),44 Cal. App. 2d 577, 
112 Pac. 2d 731). 
In addition to these provisions, the Charter of the City of Modesto (section 
905, Assembly Concurrent Resolution 31, Ch. 46, Stats. 1951) provides that no 
employee of the city shall be financially interested in a contract, sale, purchase or 
lease or transfer of real property to which rhe city is a party or be employed by or 
have an interest in any organization or fum or corporation having a contract with 
rhe city. The Charter provision further provides that an interest of less than 5% 
of rhe stock of a corporation shall not be deemed a "financial interest" under the 
section. 
There roay be some question as to which of rhe above provisions of law is 
applicable in the present case, though the provisions are essentially the same, 
with the exception that a stock interest of less than 5% is not considered culpable 
under the charter section. Since our conclusion would be the same under either 
of the provisions, it is unnecessary to discuss a possible conflict between the 
general law and the charter provision at this time. 
No mention is made in rue Charter for the deposit of public funds of the 
city and the provisions of the Government Code relating to deposit of funds by 
local agencies (sections 53630-53681) would apply (Clouse v. City of SatJ Diego, 
159 Cal. 434, 115 Pac. 573). 
~ 
~ 
The City Treasurer is authorized by sectio~ 53635 of the Government Code to 
deposit public funds in national or state banks and "money so deposited is deemed 
to be in the treasury of the local agency" (section 53636). 
The precise question presented, therefore, is whether the fact that n councilman 
is a direeror and holder of less than a 5 % interest in a bank prohibits the seIeerion 
of that bank as a depository of city funds by the City Treasurer. 
The Attorney General has held that the· fact that a person is a leRisIator does 
not prevent his entry into contracts of sale with the State of California or its 
agencies, except that he may not enter into a contract with the lcRisbture itself 
(14 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 78); that a head court house gardener who owned n 
private nursery was not disqualified from selling nursery supplies to the connty 
of which he was an employee because of the discretion vested in the county pur-
chasing agent to conclude contracts for the county (3 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 188); 
that a County Supervisor is not precluded from contracting for construction work 
with a school district since the contracts for school buildings or school construction 


92 
ATIORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
[VOl,. 21 
are let by Boards of School Trustees without control or supervision of the County 
Board of Supervisors (17 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 44). The significant fact in each 
of these opinions is the independent starus of the parry contracting on behalf of 
the governmental agency. 
The selection of the depositOry in the instant case is in the discretion of the 
Ciry Treasurer and not subject to the supervision or control of the City Council. 
It is true that the council is responsible for the appointment and removal of the 
Treasurer, but the relationship between one member of the council and the 
Treasurer is no less remote than that of a legislatOr and an agency ~f the State 
Government. 
Weare of the opinion that the public funds of the Ciry of ModestO may be 
deposited by the Ciry Treasurer in a bank in which a councilman of the ciry is a 
directOr and a stOckholder with less than 5 % of the outstanding capital stock . 
.-.... 
Opinion No. 52-175-March 5, 1953 
SUBJECT: STATE EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM-Members of, already 
retired under, are not barred from service of the State or of a contracting 
agency, provided that they meet the requirements of reinstatement from 
retirement; if such reinstatement is effected, such employee may be paid and 
accept salary or emoluments of office with the state or the contraCting agency. 
Requested by: BOARD OF ADMINISTRATION, STATE EMPLOYEES' 
RETIREMENT SYSTEM. 
Opinion by: EDMUND G. BROWN, Attorney General. 
John Morris, ,Deputy. 
The Board of Administration of the State Employees' Retirement System has 
asked whether one retired under the State Employees' Retirement System may be 
elected to office in the service of the State or of a contracting agency, and if elected, 
whether he may be paid the salary of such office. 
The conclusions reached may be summarized as follows: 
If otherwise qualified, a person retired under the State Employees' Retirement 
System is not barred from office by retirement alone, provid<.'1l that he meet the 
requirements of reinstatement from retirement; and, reinstatement effected and 
retirement allowance terminated, he may be paid and accept the salary or emolu-
ments of office. 
ANALYSIS 
SeCtion 20060 of the Government Code was amended in 1949 to read as 
follows: "Except as provided in section 21300 a person shall not be paid for any 
service rendered by him to the State or contraCting agency after his retirement is. 
effective and while he is entitled to receive a retirement allowance under this 
system."

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member who resides in a mobile home park may participate in and vote on a proposed rent stabilization ordinance that could financially benefit them, and whether the same rules apply to council members who own mobile home parks that could be negatively affected.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide immunity without specific councilmember names, but advises that disqualification depends on whether the financial effect is material and distinguishable from effects on the general public. A mobile home park owner likely does not meet the public-effect exception, suggesting disqualification would be required.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains multiple letters and supporting materials. Main advice letter dated November 24, 1982 (A-82-188) is the primary FPPC response. Earlier acknowledgment letter dated November 17, 1982 and incoming request dated November 10, 1982 are also included. Additional correspondence regarding City Treasurer conflict of interest issues and Ordinance No. 49 are included but relate to separate matters. OCR quality varies throughout document.

councilmember1s financial interest is distuinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703, copy enclosed.) 
The Ferraro-and Overstreet Opinions 
issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in 
this regard. 


Allen Grimes 
November 24, 19S2  
While we have been provided with no specific facts on the 
"public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home 
park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as 
a significant segment of the public generally. 
The numerousity 
and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear 
to be present. 
With respect to the park tenant, our advice letter to 
Paul H. Morgan, A-Sl-507, 12/2/S1, which you have not mentioned 
in your request, may provide you with some additional guidance, 
a copy is enclosed for your review. 
However, it should be 
remembered that there is no fixed percentage cut-off for 
determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public 
generally as other factors could influence the cut-off level.

CONCLUSION: Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, the possibility of financial benefit must be measured against the standards established in the Commission's regulation defining 'material financial effect' as used in Government Code Section 87103 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702). For a park tenant, subdivision (b)(3)(A) would be applicable; for a park owner, subdivision (b)(1) or (b)(2) would apply. Assuming a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, the issue turns on whether the effect on the councilmember's financial interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703). It is unlikely that a mobile home park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public generally, as the numerosity and diversity requirements do not appear to be present.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 November 24, 1982 Allen Grimes City Attorney City of Atascadero P.O. Box 749 Atascadero, CA 93423 Re: Your Advice Request of 11/10/82 Our No. A-82-188 Dear Mr. Grimes: Thank you for your recent letter requesting our assistance. Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b) (copy enclosed). However, we will endeavor to respond generally to your questions. The possibility of financial benefit to either councilmember must be measured against the standards established in the Commission's regulation defining "material financial effect" as used in Government Code Section 87103. Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 provides that guidance and a copy is enclosed. As to the park tenant, subdivision (b)(3)(A) would be applicable. As to the park owner, subdivision (b)(1) or (b)(2) would apply. Assuming that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on the councilmember(s) in question, then the issue will turn on whether the effect on the councilmember's financial interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703, copy enclosed.) The Ferraro and Overstreet Opinions issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in this regard. Allen Grimes November 24, 1982 Page 2 While we have been provided with no specific facts on the "public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public generally. The numerosity and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear to be present. With respect to the park tenant, our advice letter to Paul H. Morgan, A-81-507, 12/2/81, which you have not mentioned in your request, may provide you with some additional guidance, a copy is enclosed for your review. However, it should be remembered that there is no fixed percentage cut-off for determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public generally as other factors could influence the cut-off level. Lastly, without some specific facts we are unable to assist you further in your endeavor to distinguish among our advice letters, A-81-503, A-82-036 and A-82-063. If you would like further assistance in this regard, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901. Sincerely, Robert B. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to City of Atascadero's inquiry regarding whether council members with financial interests in mobile home parks (as residents or owners) may participate in voting on a proposed rent stabilization ordinance. The FPPC provides general guidance referencing applicable regulations and prior opinions while declining to give specific advice without identifying the councilmembers involved.
